content.full_text:
8420

A-78-1497

July 19, 1978


Mr. Peter L. Coye
Committee to Elect John Dunlap
410 Tuolumne Street
Vallejo, California 94590

Dear Mr. Coye:

     Thank you for your letter of July 5, 1978. As I advised you
over the telephone, there is no basic difference in terms of the
application of the disclosure and prohibitory provisions of the
Political Reform Act between door to door solicitation and any
other solicitation of funds.

     Records must be kept according to the standard of reasonable diligence; therefore, if you know or have reason to believe
that any donor will be contributing $50 or more to your campaign,
you should keep records of the amounts contributed and of the
required information to identify the donors.

     The requirements of the Act that apply to your activity
are explained in our Information Manual, of which a copy is
enclosed.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John Giansello
                                        Political Reform Analyst

JG:bw

78197

FPPC
JUL 19 11 40 AM '78

John Giansello                                    7-5-79
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100K St.
Sacramento, Calif.

Dear Mr. Giansello,

     I am the campaign manager for Senator
John Dunlap's re-election in the November
1978 general election. We are now planning
the campaign, and a direct door-to-door
solicitation of small contributions from
individuals in the 4th Senate District is
planned. Even though most of these small
contributions will be less than $50, all
contributions will be recorded along with
the name, address, etc. of the donor.

     Besides the normal record keeping
activities concerning contributions, are
there any provisions of the Political Reform
Act of 1974 which might pertain to such
a direct canvass for funds? Also, are
there regulations promulgated by the F.P.P.C.
which might be relevant to this sort of
campaign activity?

     Needless to say, we want to make
sure that we comply with all laws and
regulations which might pertain to a fund
raising canvass of voters. If you could
advise us on this matter we would greatly
appreciate it.

     Thank you for your time and consideration.

                                   Sincerely yours,

                                   Peter L. Coye
                                   Peter L. Coye
                                   Campaign Manager
                                   Committee to Elect John Dunlap

                                   410 Tuolumne St.
                                   Vallejo, Calif.  94590

sections.question_synthetic: What are the disclosure and recordkeeping requirements for door-to-door fundraising solicitation under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Door-to-door solicitation is subject to the same disclosure and prohibitory provisions as any other fundraising method. Records must be kept with reasonable diligence, and donors contributing $50 or more must be identified with required information documented.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; extracted question and conclusion from body text. Letter is informal response format dated July 19, 1978. References prior telephone advice. Information Manual mentioned as enclosure.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What are the disclosure and recordkeeping requirements for door-to-door fundraising solicitation under the Political Reform Act?

CONCLUSION: Door-to-door solicitation is subject to the same disclosure and prohibitory provisions as any other fundraising method. Records must be kept with reasonable diligence, and donors contributing $50 or more must be identified with required information documented.

embedding.summary: FPPC responds to a campaign committee regarding recordkeeping and disclosure requirements for door-to-door fundraising, clarifying that such solicitation is treated the same as other fundraising methods under the Political Reform Act.
